card.title: How the Panama Canal's Locks Work
card.teaser: How do you lift a 100,000-ton ship 85 feet into the air — using nothing but water and gravity?
card.coverImagePrompt: A colossal container ship wedged inside a narrow concrete lock chamber in Panama, steel gates closing behind it as turquoise water floods in, aerial cinematic view at golden hour

lessons.title: A staircase for ships
lessons.body: The Panama Canal isn't a flat ditch between two oceans. It's a staircase. Ships climb 85 feet up to a man-made lake in the middle of the country, then descend back down on the other side. And the only engine doing the lifting is gravity itself.

visual.imagePrompt: Cross-section illustration of the Panama Canal showing three locks stepping up to Gatun Lake and back down to the opposite ocean

lessons.title: What a lock actually is
lessons.body: A lock is a giant watertight box with gates at each end. A ship sails in, the gates close, and water is either let in or drained out — raising or lowering the ship with the water level. No pumps are needed for the lift itself. The water flows from higher ground, through culverts, and the ship simply floats up on it.

keyTerms.definition: The concrete basin that fills and drains to lift or lower a vessel
A massive underground water channel that feeds a lock chamber

visual.imagePrompt: Cutaway diagram of a lock chamber with a ship inside, water flooding through side culverts

lessons.title: Why gravity is enough
lessons.body: Gatun Lake sits 85 feet above sea level, held in place by the huge Gatun Dam. That elevated lake is essentially a giant reservoir of potential energy. Each time a lock fills, around 26 million gallons of freshwater flow downhill by gravity into the chamber. When the ship leaves, that water continues on to the sea. No pump could move that volume anywhere near as efficiently.

visual.imagePrompt: Gatun Lake seen from above with arrows tracing water flowing downhill through three locks into the ocean

lessons.title: Mules that aren't mules
lessons.body: Ships don't motor through the locks under their own power — the clearance is too tight. Instead, small electric locomotives called 'mules' run on tracks along the canal walls, towing vessels with steel cables. The original mules, built in 1914, cost $13,000 each. Today's Panamax ships clear the chamber walls by as little as two feet on each side.
lessons.funFact: A single transit uses about 52 million gallons of freshwater — equivalent to the daily drinking water for a mid-sized city.

visual.imagePrompt: Yellow electric mule locomotive on canal-side tracks, taut steel cable straining to guide an enormous container ship inside a lock

lessons.title: An idea older than electricity
lessons.body: Locks aren't new — Leonardo da Vinci sketched the mitered lock gate design in the 1490s, and Chinese engineers built pound locks on the Grand Canal as early as the 10th century. Panama's genius was doing it at impossible scale: moving mountains, damming a river, and then letting water do the rest. Sometimes the smartest engineering is the kind that lets nature do the work.

quiz.question: What actually lifts a ship in a lock?
quiz.explanation: The lock fills with water flowing downhill from Gatun Lake. The ship simply floats up with the rising water.

quiz.question: Why don't ships motor through the locks under their own power?
options: Panama bans engines in the canal
The chambers are too narrow for safe maneuvering
Their engines overheat at low speed
The water is too shallow for propellers
quiz.explanation: Panamax vessels have only a couple of feet of clearance on either side. Small electric 'mules' guide them through with cables.

quiz.question: Where does the water used by the locks come from?
quiz.explanation: Gatun Lake, created by damming the Chagres River, holds the water that feeds every lock transit — all of it fresh, not salt.
